speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Arthur Bjerke remembers more of early life in the Derry area, including the names of the men who homesteaded the town site. He speaks of surveying the countryside one winter with Bill Helmer for potlatch forests, of taming an outlaw horse, of the advantages of oxen for logging, of half wild cattle, and of sheep grazing in the area. He gives his impressions of Italian railroad workers and of Indians, of gypsies and tramps, and also of homesteaders' poverty and serious law -breaking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When we stopped talking last time, we were talking about Sam Piwash or Piwash?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he make a living now once he started doing his lumber business?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, he was in the logging business as long as he lived. And he done little farming up there on the hog matter. And generally, he had a bunch of them dagoes working for him. Some of them were some of his relatives. And he had one cousin now when Sam died. He's made a will so he got $25 a month as long as he lived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he make a living?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, that Sam. He was a good American even if he was a dago. Boy, you say anything about this country and you'd have a fight on your hands with old Sam.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say he sort of got a lot of scrap lumber?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say he got scrap lumber?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, that is scrap timber. That is such a timber that, well, the only thing that was good for it was to make paper out of something like that. And they wanted to cut off, see, to start a new forest. Well, they'd give it to him and he'd log it and haul it open for scrap timber. So they'd make chips out of it for paper. Of course it wasn't much but they'd make a good living.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And then he always farmed a little besides up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you find out that those Italians were killed when they were building the railroad?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, that was generally more say about the other lumberjacks that worked on the railroad. So this is more hearsay than anything else.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But they claimed there was one buried in that field that goes through Derry and one up there at Cornell.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But, God knows. You know, there were all day goes and once in a while there'd be a servant or something else, a different breed of them that'd get in with them. And that one breed would kind of have it in for him and they'd get rid of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did the World War I start?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, the World War I started, you know, putting here all of them fellas disappeared. They went back to Europe.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There were a few that stayed here that had been here long enough and learned to talk for the good.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And of course after a while someone was married and the kids were born here, you see, and they'd become citizens. So they stayed around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to know any of them, the Italians or the Bohemians that worked on the railroad when you were working on the railroad?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah, I knew several of them. And you know they weren't too bad fellas to work together with. You know they were put near like the Indians. If they took a liking to you, they were a damn good friend. And if they didn't like you, they didn't like you neither.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There was one bunch that had a station work up here. Well, we always sold eggs and butter and stuff like that to them. And once in a while we'd butcher a beef and they'd get the beef here. However, I wouldn't want to be around nicer ones than that fellow.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They were damn nice fellas.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And there used to be one up here in camp. His name was Sam Samowitz. He was kind of a good sort of a fella and he was kind of a watchmaker too. He'd always sit around and monkey and patch watches for the boys in the evening. He was a darn good fella.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And he was a section boss on all the railroad spurs around. So there was always a bunch of them working for him. And they boarded right in with the white fellas in the camp.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, they got along pretty good with them. As I say, if they liked you, by God they were good friends.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, it was the same way with the Indians. If you'd got to know an Indian and by God if they got to liking you, you didn't have a better friend than an Indian.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There were some Indians that used to camp around here. Boy, I got along good with them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And they'd always come down and get water in the well here. I'd always talk to them Indians.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Those days there were so much hawker birds around here. And they'd come up here and pick hawker birds and they'd camp all over through the woods here. Well, if there was any of them camped close by, they'd generally come down and get water out of the well. And they'd camp just right down the edge of the field.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And there used to be quite little pines along the road there, so it was pretty shady. Some of them had camped down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you ever go over to where they camped?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah. Yeah, I got along pretty good with the Indians.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the camps like then?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, they had a teepee, you know, and they had the beds all around inside. And then a little campfire on the outside.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And by gosh, you know, there was years afterwards the Indians quit coming in here. You would see for them teepees being because they had the beds. See, it would shut off the air so it would kill the grassroot. So they'd be bare. And there'd be a little bare spot in the middle. And I'd hear such a grassroot grow on, but there'd be a round circle that would be bare when they had the beds.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: You told me about fixing the wagon tongue for that Indian.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any other Indian friends?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah. Well, I was pretty well friends with all of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like to be a Indian?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Put near every one of them, you know, that camped up here. They'd always come down and I had a pond right over there across the creek. And they'd take their horses over there and water them. Got the drinking water out of the well. And you know them darn Indians, they could come with maybe fifteen, twenty horses. And you know they could herd them right in the trail, right up to the pond through the grain. And by God, they could keep them horses right in that trail. But they didn't run all over like anybody else's horses would.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: All the Indians was pretty good. And by gosh, I don't blame the Indians at all neither because when the white just took the country away from them, it was really their country to begin with.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And them Indians all come from down there on the next first reservation and down there at Lapaway.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Then from Kendrick down through that draw that was put near all Indians in early days.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And that's where there was because there wasn't much snow down in here. But then in the summer they'd come up in here and pick huckleberries and pick camels and catch a few fish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see any gypsies around here?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, there used to be a few of them too that had come through, but not many.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, them gypsies got done. But none come along. By God, you better not get out of the house and steal everything you've got.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And by God, they'd get around and want to feel of you and by God, if you want, careful and pick your pockets too.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That was quite a few of them that they picked the pockets on.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I guess they even picked the pockets on. Old niggas go up here.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Now when you see them damn gypsies come about the best way, you have to take a gun and go out and meet them and tell them to get to hell and up the road. And if they didn't get started dropping a few balls pretty close to their heels.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That was about the only way to take care of them. I don't think there's any more gypsies around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would guys come through who are just poor or beggars looking for a place to stay or a handout?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yeah, there used to be quite a few of them old fellas come along and ask for a handout once in a while. But a big lot of them, you know, they were just kind of lumberjacks. Well, they'd get a meal and someone would want to split a little wood for a meal and someone wouldn't. But they generally hike on.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No one ever had any trouble with them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They were a pretty good sort of a bunch of fellas. A big lot of them. Just being up in the woods trying to get work and couldn't get work and just trying to get out and they were broke.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Someone might have a gun or something and they'd want to borrow a few dollars on that or something like that to get out.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Maybe a jacknife or something to try to borrow for a bit or something on it.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, I said, those were pretty good, though. They didn't steal nothing.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But they were just down and out, you know. A lot of them fellas would be up in here and work. Well then they'd work and get a little money and they'd go into Spokane. Well, they'd spend their money and they'd come back. Well, maybe the camps were laid off and they couldn't get a job. Well then they'd have to kind of bum their way out.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And those days, you know, if you had a, if you were broke and you went to Spokane, if you had a few pennies, say you could go to a saloon and buy a schooner or beer and put near all of them saloons at lunch counter. Well, you'd buy a schooner or beer and then you'd go to the lunch counter and eat all what you want over there. And that was free. By God, there was lots of them old lumberjacks that had put in the winter maybe for about 15, 20 cents a day. Schooner or beer and go from one of them saloons to the other and get a free lunch.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And along Christmas, you know, they put up a hell of a lunch around them saloons.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, and around them schooner. Scandinavian saloons, they'd put up that stockfish or loot fish. And a lot of them Scandinavian lumberjacks, you know, they went for that to be tagged.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But stockfish and then what they call the flop bread. Well, we called it shingle bread. And that was thin, you know, baked on top of the stove just like a cracker but it'd be thin and crisp. Well, they'd like that and stockfish.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, they'd put in their meal from next meal but maybe they'd go to another saloon and eat. Maybe they'd get along for two bits or so a day so they didn't have to have much money because the camps started in Oakland so they could go to camp and work again.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: I always hear that there was a lot of drinking in the camps too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that like?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, they'd drink some. Not too much though. Mostly on holidays, on weekends.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: During the week there wouldn't be much drinking but there'd be quite a lot of poker plays. There used to be one fella that was up here at Helmer and then there were some fellas from Boba. They used to gamble like hell. By God, there was one stump over across Carrow Creek. They got a gambling over that one day and about $2 ,500 changed hands over a yellow pine stump over there one day. Well, Lance, some god damn geek, you know, that was smart aleck. He'd come down and hit them camps about weekends and by God, he'd take them all and he'd get the hell out.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh yes, there was quite a lot of gambling around that place. And by God, they gambled quite a lot right here in Deere too in early days.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And then over there on the Fluge River when there was a logging lot before the potluck started in, I guess that was quite a gambling place. That's what I said.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know any stories about guys gambling or about fights over gambling or any funny stories about gambling?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, not much because I always stayed away from that. I didn't have no use for that gambling.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I always stayed away from whether we were doing that.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And I never did drink whiskey.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I'd drink a glass of beer once in a while. One time we thrashed and I had to go to Moscow. Well, we'd been thrashing peas and they were all mildewed.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And there was quite a lot that had to be cleaned up around and under the feeder and a lot of peas would fall off. Well, I worked around the feeder quite a lot and of course I got quite a lot of that mildew. Well, we got through with that last job and it would be nothing dry anymore so we'd have to lay off a few days. And I had a little repairs to get with a machine so I drove over to Moscow and got them repairs and come back and get to Dury. Well, we stopped and had lunch and the sex store was with me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I said, what do you want to drink?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, he said, let's take a beer today. Well, we took a beer with our lunch and boy, I never got the damn sick of anything as I got from them mildewed peas and that beer. And you know, I think it was twenty years before they were getting me to take a beer and I don't like beer now neither.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I can take a drink of beer but that's plenty.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: I guess there was a lot of moonshining too when Prohibition came in.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, now that was quite a lot of moonshine.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yes, there was lots of moonshining going on.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I've seen one go by one time that it was sixteen gallon jugs of moonshine in there.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: I was wondering about the trails in this country, like around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember how the trails got put in in the first place?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, there's been quite a few cattle in here and sheep and there were kind of trails all over in the woods.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, when I cleared that field over there, over in the farthest end, I found a round bell in there. And that bell was marked in 1876 and I picked that up and brought it home. And my sister got to see it and, boy, she'd like to have that bell. Well, I said, you can have it. Then my nephew's wife, that'd be my niece, married. She'd seen it and, oh, she'd like to have a bell or a bell of some kind. And it went several years and, by gosh, I plowed one out over in the field. It was full of mud but I happened to see it was something. So I stopped and got off from the tractor, went back to see what it was because that was a bell and that was marked 1876. So I dug the dirt out of that so she's got that.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: Take this road that comes in here up Rush Creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did that start becoming a trail? Was that the way the trail came into the homestead?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, but of course it was just crooked all over back and forth. The first trail that come in come down over that field and went out the other way. This road you come in now from the highway, that's the road I made.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, there was just up and down hills. Just an Indian trail.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And that trail come up the meadow line and, boy, how wet that had been this spring. Mud it.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, they holler about hard times nowadays. Hell, they ain't seen nothing.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: My God, you could buy all the butter you wanted to for five cents a pound, eggs five cents a dozen, a sack of flour, forty -nine pounds for fifty cents. But you didn't have the money to buy it.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That was during the Cleveland time.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And then the wet years come, well, about the best a man could get in the harvest field was seventy -five and a dollar a day.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And then that wet year, the grain all sprouted in the field. That was about the only thing you get was a little sprouted grain.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, here there were some of them. They'd get home and they had a little money coming but couldn't get it unless a little sprouted grain. If you had any hogs or any cattle, you could take that and feed them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I know Anderson's up here. He's worked all summer and when fall comes, they had fifty cents. Well, they put that away, hid it, maybe somebody had been in and sold it or something. They never did find that fifty cents to start the winter on.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That was hard times too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they get through the winter?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, they got through. They had a cow or two and got a hold of a little flower. And a few spuds they raised, that was just about all they had to eat was a little milk and butter and bread and spuds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was this? Was this 1893 or later?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, about 92, along with 91 and 92.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it drive a lot of people out of here, a lot of the homesteaders?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, by gosh, you know, this all stayed pretty well.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But then, they couldn't file on the place. They were just squatting on the place. But there had been one survey over it that was surveyed during the 80s. But then it had to be surveyed. And they couldn't file on the place before it was surveyed. And of course, it changed it quite a little. Like over for Camp Po, there's two section corners over there, close together, and a lot of short 40s.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And of course, the same way, well, now this homestead here was four acres too big. So the dead had to buy them four acres.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, that is two acres to the 40, and the same over there, and the same 40 down below here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did a locator find this homestead for your father?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, he just located himself.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: The first place he put in the cabin was over across the creek here. But then they found out that was a school section. So he come over here and took this. And you know, those days, if you didn't live on the place, if you moved off from the place and was off for 30 days, hell, somebody else could move into your cabin.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: So they got that cabin built. And they moved up here the 17th day of March and been here all the time since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know of any troubles that homesteaders had around here with their claims getting jumped or anything like that?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, a little further east there was, well, someone that go away and other had filed homestead on them. Say, if they didn't get the money so they could file on the homestead, somebody else that last day it was open, they could go in and file on your homestead. So there was quite a few that lost their claim. And in that way they didn't have money enough to file on. And now there's two, three places up here. Fellers didn't have money to file on their place. And that went back to the state land and is still a state land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you mean there was no place they could borrow the money?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Hell, there was nobody that had the money to borrow. They were all broke.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know of any troubles that homesteaders had around here with their claims getting jumped or anything like that?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Dad, you know, didn't have any money to file on this place. But my sister, she's been working over there at McConnell. And she had a few dollars so she let Dad have and he could file on the place.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That's how he got the place here.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And this was, he filed a preemption on this place. And you could live on it for three years and you could prove up on it. And then you could go somewhere else and homestead. But Dad never got the money to prove up so Dad turned it over to homestead instead of preemption.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: If he'd had them cook a hundred dollars to file preemption, he could have filed homesteaded preemption on this and then went and took another homestead up there on the meadow. Because that was later that was open.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But he didn't have the money to prove up on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why he picked right around here? Is it because it was open around here and wasn't open other places?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, it wasn't open here but there's been bedding ground on that meadow there. And God does, you know, get the tree cut down and the logs skidded up into piles and work it over and seeded grain and that grain would grow higher than your head.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, there's no other way to cut it. It used to be a scythe but it made lots of hay.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, Dad and he'd cut with the scythe and then my younger sister and mother, they'd rake with the hand rakes and shock it.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And used a kind of a long sled with a basket rack on to haul it in.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, after a few years I got big enough and Dad fixed up the scythe for me. So I cut a lot of hay with the scythe. He made a scythe handle and he made it just the right length for them. Instead of croakers like they used them here, they were straight. And the end of the scythe handle is there on your arm and then the handhold out here and the handhold down here on top. Well, I wish lots of time I had one of them scythe handles. But they burnt up and the old buildings burnt up because there was upstairs and the old woodshed. And they were a whole lot easier to cut with than the scythe handles that we got now.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Then after a while they used to seed little rye.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And they'd cut that and make bundles out of it and thrash it. And Dalberg fixed up a kind of a grinder down there. So he'd grind it and make a kind of a fine flour of it so they'd make rye bread.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Geez.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, that rye bread was pretty good. I wouldn't mind having a loaf of that coarse rye bread now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did your father go on using the scythe?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, turns out they got the biggest part of the stumps out of the way. There was a much smaller stump. Well, he got a hold of a stump puller and we pulled a lot of the stumps and got it so that we could get room enough to get between the stumps. And then the first mower he got was a hind cut. It was behind instead of afterwards. The mower was a front cut.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They were a pretty good mower but they were awful heavy and were slow. They weren't geared very high. Your puddner had to cut the team to get up enough motion to cut.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, I run one of them mowers quite a lot.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And then I run one of them other mowers more. The front cut quite a lot. There were a better mower though. Them hind cuts, you know, you couldn't hardly raise the circle on them. They were so damn heavy to raise the circle. But the front cut, you could raise them 18, 20 inches right off from the seat with the paddle.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well then, along in 28 -30 when starting getting a tractor, I got a tractor mower. And of course that went a whole lot faster.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well the John Deere, or the International mower wasn't fast. But then I got a John Deere, that tractor mower.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Hell, I could raise the circle mower up four feet or better. Heck, I could cut 30 -35 acres a day with that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that you got a thrashing machine in those days too?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, I got the thrashing machine in 1930. And I used that for quite a few years.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, here a week ago after he was down the other time, one of the boys was down. He lives down in and around Eugene, Oregon. He worked for me six years.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He was down one evening and visited with me. He was out to see his sister. She lives down here there.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: I wanted to know more about some of the other homesteaders that were your neighbors around here. How they made out and different things that happened to them in the early days.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, Martin Magnusson. You know, after I quit my herd in Kettleboro for Hooker and Weeden about 1905, well, when I started building the railroad, Magnusson, he got a contract to furnish the company with beef for all the railroad camps. So, I got to know him. Some days I said, take two, three, four beef a day. So I got kind of in and bring in beef for him. So, heck, I knew putting every farmer from Bova to Troy. And we're down below Troy, picking up beef and bringing in. And those days, it was lots of beef that was pretty wild. Oh, we'd get some wild son of a bitch. But,

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Mrs. Magnusson's brother, he was generally with me. Lots of times we'd start in the morning and never get in before after midnight with beef if we'd strike out logs. My God, we'd have some real outlaws sometimes. Boy, they'd fight horses and everything else.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But we never had any trouble unless we brought them in. But some of them had quite a lot of trouble with it.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, then, after he quit, he started the butcher shop in Deere, and that is after the potlots quit beefing. He started the butcher shop in Deere, and I stayed abroad in quite a few cattle for him. But not very many. Then Dad sold the timber here, and he bought it 40 down on the ridge.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And we had quite a little here and we'd build the hay here and bring our cattle down on the ridge. And we'd winter them down there.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And we had enough so we could live here and enough down there. And that's all about what we had to do. We'd bring the beds and some of the eeks down there to live down there. And Dad would bring that down in the wagon. I'd bring the cattle down the first year. I'd have to have the help to drive them down because the cattle wouldn't go down. But we made it fine in Deere. After that, we'd been there. Over the winter, we fed the hay down there. Well, when they started getting green grass, we'd bring them up here and keep them up here during the summer because you could run them on the range anywhere you wanted to. And we'd live up here in the summer. And you know, after we'd been down there one year and the cattle got used to it, I'd have to watch them like the dickens and fall because when it come that time, they'd want to pull out for them on the ridge. After that, I'd have to watch them to keep them here because we were ready to move. But after that, about the only thing I'd have to do is turn them out and start them. And boy, they'd try.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother stay up here in the winter when you took the cattle down?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, she was down there too. No, see, when Dad'd take the bed and that stuff down, she went right along so she stayed there. But I'd come up and stay the night once in a while.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Especially during the hunting season, I'd come up and hunt. Maybe I'd kill a deer and bring back down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much trouble with cattle, different people getting mixed up together on the range?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, not too much.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, I kept a pretty good eye on our cattle here, so I kept them pretty well in this part down below here. Because there was nobody else that had any cattle in there.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Up that way, there was a few that had cattle, but I'd keep our cattle out of that country and keep them down below.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you went out to get cattle for the lumber camps, did you have a hard time tracking them down sometimes?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, not too bad.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, generally, the farmers hid by the cattle, you know, and tried to have them in the corral. Some biggest part of the time they'd been in the corral. Once in a while there'd be some that'd been in the canyon. We'd have to get out of the canyon, but then we'd have a little time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would you go about doing it?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Just go in and chase them out and forget them out in the open and man rope them and maybe tie them together, two and two, joke them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Then, of course, when they'd try to run out of the road, they'd hang up so they wouldn't get away very far.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: One time we got two young brothers that we had to take out of the canyon. Boy, we had a time with them, but we brought them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And they were pretty tame when we got in with them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But I don't believe there was a whole fence between Barrow Creek and Magnusson's Place left because them son of a guns, they'd go right through the fence.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And we'd have to drive them up through the fence somewhere else again and into the road. But we brought them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: In those days I didn't give a damn what kind of a horse it was neither. I could ride any horse. I didn't give a damn how wild our buck here was.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: You told me about that horse you traded with the Indian for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there any other horses that you had that you can remember, stand out, and have a good time?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well,

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: that's the only one I traded with the Indians. I traded once in a while with other people, but that would generally be tame horses. Well, I bought one out down on the range that told me that nobody could ride that horse. And it always got away every time they had it hooked up. Anyway, I bought the horse. I snubbed it to my saddle horse. And I got it home. I got it in the barn up there on the range.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I fooled around with it every day. I got along pretty good with it. But it was hard to get it down to the water. I'd put in, you have to take the saddle horse to read you out. One day I went down there. I put the saddle on there. And I fooled around. And finally I got her out.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I said, I'm going to try to ride her and see what's happening. I got on to her and that darn thing wouldn't... She just froze. She just stood there. She wouldn't move a leg.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I thought to myself, well I can fix that all right. Because I always had a set of spurs in the barn. Because they come off handy when you... After a wild cattle once in a while you'd get one of them that would lay down and you could get up and... And I'd spray them around the neck and they'd get up. I got in and put the spur on. I got on the saddle and back under the saddle. She still didn't move. Well I raced her along the shoulder and back. And boy she went into the air then. Well that was all right. She booked three, four times and boy she booked hard. And she took off running. Well I started up towards Dury. There was nine miles to Dury. I rode her into Dury. And I got off from her in Dury. And I went into the restaurant and got a cup of coffee and a piece of pie. I come out and I thought maybe I'll have a little trouble when I get on it. Well I got on it and by God you know she went off just like an old horse. Well I rode her home. I didn't hurt her at all. I rode her and she's a cool elf. After the time I come up here I put her in the barn. I give her a kind of a rubbing down. And one thing another. Miss was along before Thanksgiving. And I kept taking her out every day and ride her a couple of miles. And take her in, rub her down, cry. She got pretty nice and gentle. And Thanksgiving day I thought it would be fun to ride down because I figured all of them old timers should be down there at the Lutheran church. It was service. I rode down there. But I waited long enough just about the service had started. I rode down there and I get off. I tied you up and I went in and sit down in the back end of the church. Quite a few of the boys that I knew in there said hello to the boys that I stood down by. I didn't say anything. The research that the service was out, of course they all come out. And they say I had to settle on that out low horse. Well there wasn't one of them that would seem to ride that horse. That would be kind of fun to see.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well I stopped and talked to a lot of them. And finally it was cold out there. I said I better go home. So I went down. There was nothing. I kind of scratched her a little. I stepped into the strip and got into the saddle and I rolled up just like I was riding an old horse. Well they couldn't figure how I broke that horse in that short a time. Well I didn't have no trouble.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well I brought her up. She was kind of a slender horse.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And my God you know I broke her to jump. And I used to after cattle quite a lot. I had heard that I could drive up to any of them old rail fences. And if she could put her head over her, she'd hop over her just like that. I could drive over any damn fence in the country.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well she got so I couldn't turn her loose out in the pasture at all because she'd go and jump into the field. Well that was better feeding than that. So I kept her pretty well in the barn the time when I wasn't a user. But I broke her to drive. And I could drive her from here to Troy and back in three hours easy.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That would make about a thirty mile drive and I could make that in three hours.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Ten miles an hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a horse was she?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: She was kind of a Roman. But I think she was kind of a part of Hamilton.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the secret of being good with a horse? Huh? What's the secret of being good with a horse? Of handling a horse right?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well you take a horse is funny. I could put near all of it, get along with an outlaw horse. And the same way I could go to any place that had a mad dog that had bite. And I could go to that place and the dog wouldn't bother me. And the same way with jell -o -be jackets and bees that bite other people. But bite got bees and they'd crawl all over me but they wouldn't sting me.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There's something about that.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: We used to cut quite a few bee trees. Then they got the bee trees down. I'd generally have to take the honey out because the other fella would let it be with me. The bees would be after. I seen fellas try to come out there and by God the bees would drive them right out of the country, sting hell out of them and they'd walk all over me and wouldn't bother me.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There's something about bothering. I could get along good with them. Other people couldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's because you weren't afraid? Could that have been it?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That's one thing with a hell of a lot. You take a bee if you're afraid of them and start striking at them. They get mad. And the jell -o -dakit the same way. If you start fighting them, you get trouble on your hands.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And you take a jell -o -dakit come fast and light on you like it's going to sting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what you can do that it can't sting you?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: You just hold your breath and that jell -o -dakit can't sting through your hide.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: You can brush him off. No sir, he can't put his finger through your hide if you hold your breath.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There's a lot of them that don't know that.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And them big ball -faced hornets was the same way.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And big ball -faced hornets, when they'd hit hard.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: I had one of them hit me from behind that I didn't see him coming. Boy, he damn near knocked me over.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: There's been one a -monkeyin' around out here, but out here on the forest, sitting out there. He'd walk all over on my hand or arm. He wouldn't bite me, but he'd suck the moisture off from the forest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have cattle? Did they have many head of cattle back when the lumber camps were going and you were getting cattle for the camps?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Not too many cows. That one, none of them had too many cows.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, they took me to see for a whole mule. That was just about all they had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But there's a lot more pasture in this country than there is, like say, around Genesee or down around in there, isn't there?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, there was quite a little more pasture, you know. There were some years, them pliers from down below would bring cattle up. And then generally the sheep men would come in with big herds of sheep. And of course, they'd raise hell with the pasture. Because when the sheep went over it, cattle wouldn't pasture after where the sheep had been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people around here have sheep?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, not too many. We used to have a few.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, there'd be a brush, a piece of ground, and they'd generally fence it in and put sheep in there. Well, the sheep would know the bark off from the brush and pick the leaves off from the brush. And in a couple, three years they'd kill the brush. So it'd die.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: So it was easier to clear.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did these sheep herds come from that pastured up here then?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They come from down in the Big Bang country.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: One part out fit with the McGregor's. And then the camel out fit.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But they wouldn't stay but a couple, three days. Then they'd keep them open on. Well, they could move them along the county roads.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But then after years, when the cars started going, they couldn't move them so much that they'd have to truck them more or less. Then they'd have to go further back in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make the guys that had cattle mad that the sheep would come through and graze it out?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, you know there was one law. You have a piece of ground and you could keep the sheep two miles away from your piece of ground.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They couldn't come in closer than two miles of your place unless you let them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Like some forest that has cattle up in here, you know, they'd leash places. Well, they'd bring the cattle in and they could keep the sheep away two miles. Well, then they'd go over here and waste maybe three or four miles and leash another place. Well, they could keep the sheep away there. So they'd have quite a piece to pasture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean they wouldn't leave enough room for the sheep to get in?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, finally, they made a kind of a law that there'd be one streak of country that the sheep could use and the cattle could mend the other part. Then, of course, it didn't bother. We didn't get any more trouble with the sheep over on this side of the hill. They always come back on the other side of the hills further towards the plumes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember some more about some of the other homesteaders that were around in this area?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, Halsett, you know, they set it up here and they were four boys.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He was kind of, oh man, Halsett was just kind of sort of blind on one side. He couldn't see much on the other. They homesteaded up there and they had four boys. The first kids that went up there on the Neger Schoolhouse were them four boys, I and my sister, and Hawk and Peterson. And the three Neger kids, there was ten of us in Old Halt the first year.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And the next year, the next year it was two Thompson kids and the Bredloch kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did some of these other families do? Did they have any troubles that you can remember?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, no, they got along pretty good. Like Halsett's up here. He generally stayed home because he couldn't see good. And Mrs. Halsett, she worked down on the sawmill, cooked down there for the mill crew. And the oldest one of the boys was along and he was kind of a flunkied out.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And those days, you know, put near everything the skidded logs with was worthless. There were no horses, no horse kiddin', no big bugs to skid those.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me what these oxen used to be like to use on the teams? Can you tell me what these cattle used to be like to use on the teams?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, there were cattle. I don't know. The cattle was quite a little wider than those days than they are now. They were more of a wild breed. They came down like they do now. Every once in a while, God, you'd find one that'd fight just like a damn tiger when you'd get after the chasin'.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Now they're putting their own cattle in pretty tame.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they used to be like to pull the wagons, to do the farm work?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said that they used the oxen before they had horses.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, you know, they had a yoke over their neck. And one had a big staple in the yoke and just a chain to pull the wagons. And when they used to wagon, they had a slip in the yoke that they put the tongue in so they couldn't run ahead. And now like plowing, they just hooked that cable onto the plow and the same thing when there were skidding logs with them. They just had a skidding tongue in the end of the chain that they'd hook on the end of the log. A bull was a whole lot better to skid log with than horse. You could go in the damn brushier's patch with them. That damn big joke. And a spinal bullet bent over trees as big as your arm.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And you just had the damn chain and that tongue to hook on the log and then skied them out with it. All you had was a switch. You didn't have a line or nothing. It was just G and Hall to guide them.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: G and Hall was right and left. Them bulls were so they'd understand that good.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Maybe if there was a big log, they'd put on four, maybe six bulls to pull them out. Then they'd haul out big logs.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: No, put in all their sawmills down here they used wool. And then they'd just log for the mills in the winter and then skid out to the schedule with the bulls. And then a lot of them they'd use horses and haul their logs from there on into the mill. Haul them on slaves.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: That went pretty good.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And the mills generally had big ponds. Well, they'd empty them in the fall and there was a guy riding in the pond and on the road. And God they'd take them logs up higher than the ceiling in there. Then in the spring when the water had come, they'd kind of float them logs. And they'd float them down and the mill was right down at the end of the pond. Well, they'd drag them up a drum way and right up next to the carriage and so on, see. And the water would take the frost out of the logs so they'd saw parties.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And I'm glad they saw me. The end of the head about 20, 25 men.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: They saw them and then they'd have tracks up and they'd run the lumber out on lump cars and then they'd pile the lumber along the tracks to let it dry. And then when it was dry, they'd haul it back on them dump cars and into the mill and run it through the plainer and plainer.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And how they'd haul a lot of that lumber.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Eight dollars a thousand, six to eight dollars a thousand. Maybe you'd have the logs about two.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Maybe you'd have to take out the logs for lumber and then maybe haul it to Moscow and sell the damn lumber for about ten dollars a thousand. So you see it was damn fast in making money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how the town of Derry got started?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know how it got started?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, there was a fella by the name Bill Derry and there were some stores right down in below where Derry is now.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And Bill Derry, he had a brother. He was kind of a foreman for the crew when they were surveying the railroad and one thing another.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And I don't know, they just bought that place from Braylock and then they decided to make a town out of it. And finally they got it surveyed out of the town lots and Braylock and that is a few hundred. And then in McGowan, of course they were potlatch men. They come up there to kind of run things and sell lots. They got a selling lot and main town of Derry is on the Braylock place. Well in part of Derry is on Three Homestead. It's on the Braylock place and the Roundtree place and the Brett Cooks place. The screw house and that up there is on the Brett Cooks place. And this end of Derry is on the Roundtree place.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And Brett Cooks girls, they had two girls. They went to school the second year of school. And their name was Della and Mae.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well that Cooks, he was quite a booze fighter and finally he sold the place to some other fellow. And he sold it to the Potlatch Lumber Company and Potlatch Lumber Company started a town. Well Braylock, he went down and he started a story down there by Joel. This kind of Joel and they call that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What in hell was? Cornwell?

speaker: Speaker 2
words: Cornwell Howell.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Cornwell. Cornwell Howell was spotted this way. Cornwell is where Braylock started. And he had one boy that growed up there and he got to be a kind of a tough one like the old man Drockard. And he died and the old man died and the oldest girl died. I think the oldest, that is the next to the oldest girl, I think she lives down there around Howell some where now. She got married down there. I know she was there for a good many years. I can't think of the name. But I know she was there a few years ago because she was up to Derry and I happened to meet her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Derry get to be a regular town overnight or did it take quite a while for it to grow up?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh it growed pretty fast. Oh yeah. Yes there was quite a few stories that got started. Got a saloon up there. Drug store. Finally we got a doctor there. Oh yes we had both a doctor and dentist and shoemaker shop. Oh it was quite a place at one time. And you know when there was, when there were building roads there was lots of lumberjacks that built shacks there and left there that were working on the railroad.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And of course when they went through with the building the railroads they went other places. And Helmer had quite a lot of others up there.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And Camp Six was kind of a headquarter for putlers up there. That had quite a lot of people up there. Helmer had worked over at Camp Six. But hell there was over seventy -five or a hundred men over there working up them canyons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do after you got done working on the railroad? Me?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh I, I mostly, mother was getting pretty old. I mostly stayed at home and kind of looked after cattle. And we always had ten or twelve cows. And then I done quite a lot of carpenter work around. I built quite a few houses. I started with houses in Dury.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And there's several houses in Helmer, some in Boba, some down on the Red.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: After mother died I got ready so I had only one cow. But she was an awful good cow. I could turn her loose, her and the calf, and go out and work. And she'd put me over stay right around the place. When I'd come home she was putting her clothes in so I could call her. And she'd come in and I'd put the calf up and maybe a quart or two and get a little meal and turn her up. And she'd always work around home.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And we'd ask about time to milk her and I'd call her in. And I'd go to the milk, maybe a quart or two or maybe turn the calf on and let him take the rest of it. Then turn him in his basket and she'd just walk out and tease around close to home. And I had that cow for years. Christ, she must have been eighteen, nineteen years old when I sold her.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And the butcher in Boba, he'd always come down and wanted to know if I'd have a calf from that cow. He bought that calf for nearly every year because that calf was hog dressed about four hundred and fifty pounds in the fall. After the champagne.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He said that's the only place you could get any goat beef when you'd get that calf from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will you tell me about the work that you did with Bill Helmer?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well, he come in here, that was before Mother died. And he was running out the line and he come to the house and he wanted to stay here some. Well, Mother is getting pretty old so it's kind of hard for her to do much cooking.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: But I said we'll ask Mother and see what she's got to say. So we come over to the house and talk to her. Mother says, well she says, alright you can stay if you eat what we eat. Well it was alright, he'd just stay about five days a week. So he come down early in the fall and he was just alone. He said I need somebody. So we came down early in the fall and he was just alone. Because he was running out kind of the section lines and getting the section corner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So we kind of, he says, that's what you want to work with?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, I says, but I can't put in like long days. Well, he says, I got quite a lot of book work to do and one thing and another. He says we wouldn't put in too long.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Well I'd get up and do the chores for the early morning and turn the cows out for water and pay them in the manger so they could go out. We'd go out in the woods, we'd run lines, it was about two thirty, three o 'clock in the afternoon. And well Bill says we'll go in now. Well we'd go in and I could do the chores and Bill came and he'd work on his books a little.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And by that time, Mother would have breakfast, supper, and always carry a sandwich with us out in the woods. Well we'd just mark out the old section corners. You could still see them had been run in all the days. So we worked them out and marked the corner trees up high so we could see them before the snow got too big. And then when the snow got big, then we started running the railroad track because the snow got high so we didn't have to bend down the biggest part of the brush so we could look over the biggest part of the brush. Well then cut stakes and put down the stakes about every fifty feet. Well then we wanted the railroad track and run out the crooks and curbs. So we worked on that all the winter.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: So I done pretty good that winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Bill Helmer like?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Ah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: He was a pretty dark, skinny, some, he wasn't too heavy a man. And by God that's how I could travel in the woods too. They all told me you'll never be able to keep up with Bill Helmer. I was pretty good myself after the snow got deep. We used snowshoes.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And of course we'd have a trail broken and coming in or going out in the morning. Well then we had the snowshoes trails to work on so we'd get along pretty good. No I got along pretty good with Bill Helmer. And that spring when he had the surveying done right in the neighborhood all around here, so I had to move. He said to me, I wish it just, it could have went better. I got along with you just about as good as any one. Because I never could have lost anything. And I was pretty handy with the compass myself. When he'd get tired of using it, by God I'd use it. So we kind of changed off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say that most of the work you did was a re -survey? You say most of the work you did was surveying it over again and you had the old section lines to go by?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Yeah, we had to go over them because there was pieces of timber, see, that they bought from the farmers and other from state land. So they had to keep track of that. So they had to keep the state land and what they had bought from farmers suffered.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: And some of it they had to keep in bars. So it had to be kept apart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the woods like where you were surveying them? What was the timber like there before it was cut?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Oh, it was pretty open timber because the Indians used to burn it. But it started growing up again before the potlots got logging. So it got quite a lot of rust. But when our first command, remember, you could drive foot near any horse with that team and wagon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were you working from here, the two of you? When was it?

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Surveying. Well, we worked all over on a mess township clean, clean up there on the We laid out all those railroad tracks here that winter.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: Clean over on Cragwick and over on where Camp 4 is.

speaker: Arthur Bjerke
words: On this side of Camp 4 there is a bunch of short forges in there. They are wide enough one way but they are too short the other way. And it's Camp 4.
